title: "Confirmed Correct on the Server" Is Not "Confirmed Visible to the User" — Every Caching Layer Between Them Can Independently Hide a Real Fix
problem: Victor reported Chinese missing from the language FAB on the dossier page. Across one continuous session, four independent root causes were found and fixed, each confirmed correct on GitHub's main branch at the time — and the symptom persisted through all but the last: (1) widgets/fab-lang.js never read its own build-time-baked window.VEX_SUPPORTED_LANGS global, falling through to a stale localStorage cache or network fetch instead (PR #79); (2) that network fetch's own cache-busting VERSION constant had never been bumped since introduction, so jsdelivr's CDN kept serving a stale index.json indefinitely regardless of what changed on main (PR #82, generalized into an auto-incrementing scheme in PR #83); (3) once the dossier was wired to load its God Script directly (bypassing jsdelivr for that page entirely, PR #84), a newly-activated Service Worker could install a new version but never signaled the already-open tab to use it (PR #86); (4) even with that signal wired up, the Service Worker's own background update CHECK is throttled by the browser to roughly once per 24 hours, so reloading the page — even a hard reload — did not reliably trigger a fresh check at all, meaning the fix from (3) had nothing to react to (PR #87, confirmed only after Victor pushed back and asked for an actual reproduction instead of another assumption-based patch). A separate instance (Codex, PR #89) later found two more root causes this session's own investigation never reached because it scoped to one page: (5) the localStorage cache in widgets/fab-lang.js's STANDALONE fallback path (any page still loading the widget directly from the CDN instead of a God Script) short-circuited the network fetch entirely on a cache hit — meaning any such page could freeze on a stale language list forever, independent of every fix above, since none of them touch the standalone path; (6) pages/specimen-architectural-wisdoms.html had a hand-authored `window.VEX_SUPPORTED_LANGS = ['en', 'ja']` inline script — because PR #79's own fix makes that exact global win first, this hardcoded fake permanently shadowed every other fix in the chain for that one page specifically.
exploration: each of the four fixes was independently correct and independently verifiable on GitHub's main branch (via git log, git show, raw.githubusercontent.com bypassing any CDN) at the moment it shipped — the mistake each time was treating 'confirmed correct on the server' as sufficient evidence that the live, already-open browser tab would show it, without accounting for what sat between the two

the four caching layers found were structurally different from each other (a runtime JS fallback path never wired to a build-time global; a hand-maintained CDN cache-busting query string; a Service Worker's Cache Storage API; a browser's own throttled background update-check schedule) — meaning no single fix, however thorough, could have closed all four at once, and assuming 'the caching problem' was one problem rather than a stack of independent ones was itself part of the pattern

PR #86 shipped on correct reasoning about Service Worker lifecycle semantics (skipWaiting/clients.claim/controllerchange) without reproducing the actual failure first — it was necessary but not sufficient, and would have shipped as 'the' fix if Victor had not explicitly tested it live and pushed back asking for simulation rather than another assumption

building a local Playwright-driven simulation (a small HTTP server able to swap between an 'old' and 'new' deployed version mid-test, driving the real unmodified widget file) reproduced the exact reported symptom in under an hour, definitively confirmed PR #86 alone was insufficient, and let the actual missing piece (an explicit registration.update() call) be verified fixed before claiming so — the same 'render it, don't just reason about it' discipline this repo's culture already names for browser-visible bugs, applied here to a caching/lifecycle bug instead of a rendering bug

PR #89 found what this session's own investigation missed by scoping the fix to the one page Victor reported (the dossier) rather than to the widget and pattern in general: a bug fixed on the page you found it on is not the same claim as the bug fixed everywhere that shape of bug exists — the God-Script-wiring fix (PR #84) solved the dossier by moving it off the standalone CDN path entirely, which incidentally meant the standalone path's own independent cache-short-circuit bug (found in PR #89) was never exercised or noticed during this session's testing

the hand-authored `window.VEX_SUPPORTED_LANGS` global on specimen-architectural-wisdoms.html is a new instance of an already-named class of mistake (docs/culture.md's 'never hand-author what a generator should own') showing up in a place this repo had not previously considered: not a hand-edited generated FILE, but a hand-forged runtime GLOBAL impersonating the exact build-time contract PR #79's fix trusts most — a reminder that a 'never hand-author X' rule has to be checked against every shape X can take, not just the one instance that prompted the rule
decision: No single infrastructure change resolves this pattern — caching-layer staleness is not a bug to patch once, it is a class of bug this system will keep producing as more layers (CDN, browser HTTP cache, Service Worker Cache Storage, any future layer) get added between 'the server has the fix' and 'the user's browser renders it.' Standing practice going forward: when a fix touches anything a browser might cache (a CDN-fetched asset, a Service-Worker-precached resource, a localStorage-persisted value, an HTTP-cached response), do not declare it resolved on the strength of 'confirmed correct on main' alone. Either verify the actual browser-visible behavior end-to-end (a real or simulated reload cycle, not a source-code read), or explicitly flag in the PR body that live-browser caching behavior is unconfirmed and name which layer could still be hiding it. Additionally: when a bug is fixed by moving ONE affected page onto a different, more-trustworthy code path (as PR #84 did), explicitly check whether any OTHER page still uses the old path and is still exposed to the original bug — fixing the instance you were shown is not the same as fixing the shape of bug, and the fix's own regression tests should assert the general property (e.g. 'no hand-authored page sets this global'), not just the one page's specific behavior.
lesson: A codebase with N independent caching layers between 'the fix shipped' and 'the user sees it' can produce up to N rounds of 'still not visible' before the actual last layer is found — and each round can look, in isolation, like a fully-diagnosed, fully-verified fix, because each one WAS correct for the layer it addressed. The tell that you're mid-stack rather than done is the symptom recurring after a fix that was itself correctly reasoned and server-verified. When that happens, the right move is not a fifth theory — it's reproducing the actual user-visible lifecycle (a simulation, a real device, a real reload cycle) to find out definitively whether you're looking at a new layer or an incomplete fix to the one you thought you'd closed. Server-side correctness and client-visible correctness are different claims requiring different evidence, and caching is exactly where the two can silently diverge for arbitrarily long. A second, related tell surfaced only after this lesson was first written: fixing the bug on the one page it was reported on can leave the same bug fully intact on every other page that shares the vulnerable code path, especially when the fix works by moving the reported page OFF that path rather than fixing the path itself — always ask 'does this fix the pattern, or just this instance,' and check every other consumer of the same widget/global/contract before calling the investigation closed.
impact: Five merged/pending PRs (#79, #82/#83, #84, #86/#87, #89), all labeled bug for retraceability. No single code change generalizes this — it's a standing verification discipline for any future change that touches a browser-cacheable asset in this repo, named here so the next instance recognizes the pattern on fix #2 instead of shipping fix #4 believing each one alone was the answer, and checks every other consumer of a shared widget/global before declaring a fix complete.
